content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662          322-5660             322-5901            322-6441

November 7, 1984


Thomas J. Harron
City Attorney
City of Chula Vista
276 Fourth Avenue
Chula Vista, CA 92010

Re: Our File No. A-84-281

Dear Mr. Harron:

     This letter is sent to confirm our recent telephone
conversations concerning the general application of the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ The basic
conflict of interest statute of the Act provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
to know he has a financial interest.

     Section 87100.

     This section has never been interpreted to mean that a
public official may not have certain financial interests. We
advise public officials that they are not prohibited from owning
property or operating a business. However, a public official
must abstain from any governmental decision which could
materially affect that property or business.2/ Thus, whether

_______________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code. I have enclosed copies
of our Guide to these provisions.

2/ An official must also abstain from decisions which
could materially affect a source of income to that official of
$250 or more within the 12 months preceding the decision.
Section 87103(c).

Thomas J. Harron
November 7, 1984
Page 2


a public official has a financial interest in a decision which
requires his disqualification depends on the facts and
circumstances surrounding the particular decision, I would also
like to emphasize that the statute should not be interpreted to
imply that there has been any type of violation or impropriety
when an official disqualifies himself from a decision because he
has a financial interest in one which could be affected by the
decision. An official only has a conflict of interest when he
goes ahead and participates in the decision or attempts to
influence the decision. If he disqualifies himself from the
decision, then he has complied with the statute, and no stigma
of impropriety should attach.

If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,


Diane Maura Fishburn
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:plh
Enclosures: Guide to the Political Reform Act
Proposed Regulation 18704.3 for your
review

sections.question_synthetic: What is the general application of conflict of interest provisions under the Political Reform Act, and what financial interests are public officials prohibited from having?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Public officials are not prohibited from owning property or operating a business, but must abstain from any governmental decision that could materially affect those interests or sources of income of $250 or more. Disqualifying oneself from such decisions constitutes compliance with the statute and does not imply impropriety.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present; letter confirms prior telephone conversations. Question and conclusion synthesized from body text. File number appears partially redacted (A-84-[?]).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the general application of conflict of interest provisions under the Political Reform Act, and what financial interests are public officials prohibited from having?

CONCLUSION: Public officials are not prohibited from owning property or operating a business, but must abstain from any governmental decision that could materially affect those interests or sources of income of $250 or more. Disqualifying oneself from such decisions constitutes compliance with the statute and does not imply impropriety.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises the City Attorney of Chula Vista on the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, clarifying that public officials may have financial interests but must disqualify themselves from decisions that could materially affect those interests.
